_meta.description: Single source of truth for all editable copy on the Affordability for All site.
_meta.author: Michael Green II
_meta.notes: Edit this file to update any text on the site. Pages that read from this file will auto-populate on load.

site.title: Affordability for All & The Five Fights
site.subtitle: Mayor Karen Bass
site.headerTitle: Mayor Bass: Affordability for All & The Five Fights
site.overviewBody: Los Angeles is one of the least affordable cities in America. Mayor Bass is not fighting this on one front. She is fighting five simultaneous battles, each essential, all interconnected. Together they form a complete strategy. Separately they get lost.

fights.name: Earn More
fights.subtitle: Wages & Workforce
fights.wheelDescription: Bass built a floor under LA wages. She brokered landmark labor agreements, signing minimum wage laws, and enforced living wage standards at city contractors.
fights.campaignDescription: Working Angelenos deserve wages that keep up with what it actually costs to live here. Bass brokered agreements, signed ordinances, and enforced standards.

fights.name: Build More
fights.subtitle: Housing Production
fights.wheelDescription: ED1 streamlined approvals for 100% affordable housing. Adaptive Reuse converts vacant buildings into homes. Bass inherited 50 years of underbuilding.
fights.campaignDescription: LA underbuilt housing for fifty years. Bass dismantled the bureaucratic barriers that made it impossible to catch up.

fights.name: Save More
fights.subtitle: Utilities & Tax Credits
fights.wheelDescription: DWP savings. Free tax prep that puts thousands back. Utility relief for seniors. Public power that keeps rates lower than any private utility in the state.
fights.campaignDescription: Every dollar Angelenos earn should go further. Lower utility costs, tax credits most people do not know exist, and savings programs that stretch every paycheck.

fights.name: Care More
fights.subtitle: Families, Homelessness & Safety Net
fights.wheelDescription: FamilySource. Inside Safe. Youth programs. Eviction defense. Maternal health. The safety net that catches people before they fall and lifts them when they do.
fights.campaignDescription: The programs that catch people before they fall and lift them when they do. Families, youth, homelessness, eviction defense, veterans, and maternal health.

fights.name: Protect More Rights
fights.subtitle: Tenant Rights & Housing Stability
fights.wheelDescription: Renters are the majority of Angelenos. Bass signed the first RSO overhaul in 40 years, funded eviction defense through ULA, and built a legal shield for tenants facing displacement.
fights.campaignDescription: Protecting tenants from displacement, funding eviction defense, and enforcing the strongest renter protections LA has seen in a generation.

stats.label: households served by Right to Counsel

campaigns._note: One featured campaign per fight. Tagline plus three bullets. Awareness and adoption focus.

earn.name: WorkSource Centers
earn.tagline: If you are looking for work, or better work, there is a free center near you.
bullets: The City of Los Angeles operates 14 WorkSource Centers offering free career counseling, job training, resume help, and placement services to adults, veterans, returning residents, and anyone navigating a job transition. Services are available in Armenian, Farsi, Korean, Russian, Spanish, and Tagalog.

WorkSource Centers are connected to the city's Olympic, World Cup, and FIFA procurement pipelines through 2028, meaning the next three years represent an unusually large window for Angelenos to enter new industries and secure contracts they would not otherwise reach.
Most Angelenos who qualify have never walked through the door. Find your nearest center and walk in this week at ewdd.lacity.gov.

build.name: ADUs
build.tagline: The fastest way to add housing in Los Angeles is already on your property.
bullets: California law now allows most homeowners to build an accessory dwelling unit with four-foot side setbacks, no parking requirement, and a permit decision within 60 days. LADBS's Standard Plan Program offers pre-approved architectural plans that can cut approval time significantly and reduce design costs compared to custom plans.

ADUs cost less to build than any other new housing type because they require no new land purchase. They generate rental income, provide housing for family members, and increase property value on the same lot you already own.
Eligibility depends on your property's zoning and existing structures. Use ZIMAS at zimas.lacity.org to check your property and visit adu.lacity.gov to start the process.

save.name: Low-Cost Auto Insurance & EITC
save.tagline: There is money waiting for you. Here is how to claim it.
bullets: California's Low-Cost Auto Insurance program covers eligible drivers for $401 per year, with 10/20/3 liability coverage. To qualify, your household income must be at or below 250 percent of the federal poverty level and your driving record must have no more than one point. Seventy-three percent of enrollees had no insurance before signing up. Ask about it at any FamilySource or WorkSource Center.

If your household qualifies, you may be able to claim up to $11,802 in combined federal EITC and CalEITC tax credits for tax year 2025. Free Tax Prep LA filed over 17,400 returns in 2024 and returned $21.4 million to low-income Angelenos. Forty-five locations. No cost to file.
Both programs are available through the city's 19 FamilySource Centers and 14 WorkSource Centers. No appointment needed to ask a navigator what you qualify for.

care.name: Mayors for Moms
care.tagline: Black mothers in this city are dying at three to four times the rate of white mothers. That is not inevitable.
bullets: LA County's AAIMM initiative, run through the Department of Public Health, funds doulas, group prenatal care, and community health workers for pregnant women in the highest-risk communities. Most women who qualify do not know it exists.

New York City's Citywide Doula Initiative has served over 3,245 mothers since 2022 with no maternal deaths reported among participants and reduced rates of C-section and preterm birth among Black and Hispanic participants compared to citywide averages. Mayor Bass is building a national coalition of mayors to replicate and scale that model.
If you are pregnant or know someone who is, connect now with LA County's maternal health programs at publichealth.lacounty.gov before the next prenatal appointment.

protect.name: Tenant Anti-Harassment Ordinance
protect.tagline: If your landlord is trying to push you out, that is not just wrong. In Los Angeles, it is against the law.
bullets: The Tenant Anti-Harassment Ordinance makes it illegal for landlords to use pressure tactics to force tenants out of their homes. This covers all residential renters in the City of Los Angeles, regardless of whether the unit is rent-stabilized. Illegal tactics include lockouts, utility shutoffs, construction disruption, and verbal intimidation designed to make you leave.

Most tenants being harassed do not know what is happening to them has a legal name or that the city will act on a complaint. Over 16,000 households have filed TAHO complaints since the program launched. The ordinance is enforceable and ULA funding supports ongoing outreach and enforcement.
If your landlord is doing this to you, file a complaint with LAHD at housing.lacity.gov or call 866-557-7368. You do not have to leave. You have rights and the city will back them up.

executiveDirectives.title: Making Ends Meet
executiveDirectives.subtitle: Reducing the Cost of Living for Angelenos
executiveDirectives.summary: Auto-enroll eligible households into LADWP EZ-SAVE and Metro LIFE, launch a Tri-Share childcare feasibility study, build a neighborhood market pilot, and coordinate a national Cost of Living Mayor's Conference.
background: Utility costs, childcare, and transportation represent the three largest non-housing expenditures for low-to-moderate income households in Los Angeles, routinely exceeding $8,000 per household before subsidies.
LADWP's EZ-SAVE program and Metro's LIFE program both exist and are funded, yet hundreds of thousands of eligible Angelenos remain unenrolled because neither program uses proactive enrollment.

Center-based infant care in Los Angeles runs $1,800 to $2,500 per month. Michigan's Tri-Share program, splitting costs between employer, employee, and state, has saved participating families an average of $464 per month since 2021.
Mayor Bass has previously chaired the USCM Task Force on Homelessness and used that platform to produce documented change in federal HUD policy. The same convening power applies to affordability.

sections.title: Section 1: Automated Enrollment in Utility and Transit Assistance
sections.body: The Department of Water and Power and the Los Angeles Housing Department shall, within 60 days, establish an automated enrollment protocol for eligible households into LADWP EZ-SAVE and Metro LIFE. The City shall use existing administrative data from LA County DPSS, HACLA, and municipal utility records to pre-identify eligible households. Eligible households shall receive direct notification with clear opt-out mechanisms. DWP shall project aggregate household savings within six months. Note: the water discount component of EZ-SAVE was ended by court ruling in July 2023; savings projections reflect electricity and sewer benefits only.

sections.title: Section 2: Childcare Affordability Study
sections.body: EWDD, in coordination with CIFD and DCFS, shall conduct a feasibility study on the Tri-Share childcare model. The study shall evaluate regulatory compliance under California childcare licensing, cost efficiency, and scaling potential. Financial modeling shall include fees generated under ED 17 if passed by Council, property tax increment financing, and philanthropic capital. The study shall identify 3-5 neighborhoods with the highest childcare access gaps and target families earning between 200 and 400 percent of the federal poverty level. Final recommendations due to the Mayor by November 30, 2026.

sections.title: Section 3: Neighborhood Market Feasibility
sections.body: The Mayor's Office of Economic Development, in coordination with the LA Food Policy Council and EWDD, shall evaluate the feasibility of a subsidized neighborhood market pilot in three high food-insecurity neighborhoods. The study shall assess models from peer cities, identify potential sites using existing city-owned or underutilized commercial space, and propose financing mechanisms including USDA Community Food Projects grants and philanthropic capital. Preliminary findings due September 1, 2026.

sections.title: Section 4: National Cost of Living Coordination
sections.body: The Mayor shall leverage her position with the U.S. Conference of Mayors to advance federal and state policy interventions addressing utility affordability, childcare accessibility, and transportation cost burdens. A working group of peer mayors shall be convened by June 1, 2026. A federal legislative package shall be drafted by August 1, 2026. A USCM resolution on municipal utility rate affordability shall be introduced by November 2026.

executiveDirectives.implementation: Departments referenced in this Directive are authorized to reallocate existing resources, modify procurement timelines, and coordinate across agencies as necessary. Budget implications reported to the Mayor's Office within 30 days.
executiveDirectives.reporting: Quarterly integrated progress reports to the Mayor. First comprehensive report due July 15, 2026.

executiveDirectives.title: Production Economy
executiveDirectives.subtitle: Housing and Jobs for Los Angeles
executiveDirectives.summary: Extend emergency permitting speed to all affordable housing, establish One-Stop Centers and a Department Strike Force, and conduct a feasibility study for a local film and TV production incentive tied to local hire.
background: Of the approximately 31,700 affordable housing units approved under ED1 since December 2022, only about 4,993 have received building permits. The bottleneck is permitting, not planning.

Emergency Executive Order 1, issued after the Palisades fire, proved the city can process permits three times faster when institutional will exists. The question is whether that speed is only available after a disaster.

LA County's motion picture and sound recording industry lost approximately 42,000 jobs between 2022 and 2024, a workforce reduction of nearly 30 percent, as productions relocated to jurisdictions with more competitive incentive structures.
The California Film Commission administers Film Tax Credit Program 4.0 at $750 million annually through June 2030. Several California cities have supplemented this with local incentive programs.

sections.title: Section 1: Affordable Housing Permitting Acceleration
sections.body: LADBS, DCP, and LAHD shall, within 45 days, develop a proposal to extend the permitting speed of EO1 to all affordable housing projects with 30 percent or more units at or below 60 percent AMI. Affordable Housing One-Stop Centers shall be established in each APC jurisdiction within 90 days. A permanent Department Strike Force with 30-day permit reviews, two-business-day inspections, and simultaneous department clearances shall be the operating standard. One Affordable Housing Navigator per APC jurisdiction shall guide qualifying projects from application through certificate of occupancy. A Fee Deferral and Installment Program shall allow qualifying projects to defer impact fees to certificate of occupancy, consistent with California Government Code Section 66007.

sections.title: Section 2: Local Film and Production Incentive Feasibility Study
sections.body: The Mayor's Office of Economic Development, EWDD, and the CLA shall conduct a feasibility study on a City of Los Angeles film and television production incentive program. The study shall evaluate direct subsidies, permitting fee waivers, below-market rates for city-owned facilities, and tax credit structures requiring state enabling legislation. Any proposed incentive shall include a local hire requirement for below-the-line crew positions, consistent with the market participant doctrine. The study shall analyze at least five peer jurisdictions including New York City, Georgia, the UK, Canada, and New Mexico. Final recommendations due December 1, 2026.

executiveDirectives.implementation: Departments referenced in this Directive are authorized to reallocate existing resources, modify procurement timelines, and coordinate across agencies. Budget implications reported to the Mayor's Office within 30 days.
executiveDirectives.reporting: Quarterly integrated progress reports to the Mayor. First comprehensive report due 90 days after issuance.

executiveDirectives.title: Community Anchors
executiveDirectives.subtitle: Neighborhood Business Networks for a Stronger City
executiveDirectives.summary: Create a voluntary Community Anchor designation for small businesses, connecting them to city services, CIRCLE crisis response, FamilySource referrals, and an expedited procurement pathway.
background: Small business owners across LA report that interactions with unhoused individuals represent one of their most significant day-to-day operational challenges, and that calling LAPD is frequently misaligned with the outcomes they actually want.

CIRCLE, the Mayor's 24-hour unarmed crisis response program, has handled over 14,000 diverted incidents and connected hundreds of Angelenos to services, yet awareness among small business owners remains low.

The city's 10 BusinessSource Centers and 14 WorkSource Centers provide free technical assistance and workforce services but remain siloed from the community-level relationships small businesses maintain.

Peer cities have demonstrated that networked, neighborhood-level small business programs connecting businesses to public agencies and nonprofits create measurable improvements in business stability and community safety.

sections.title: Section 1: Community Anchor Designation Program
sections.body: EWDD, in coordination with MOCS and CIFD, shall design and launch a voluntary Community Anchor designation program. Eligibility shall prioritize locally owned businesses operating for at least two years in high-need neighborhoods. Designated businesses receive a named point of contact within MOCS, EWDD, and CIFD. MOCS shall provide direct access to CIRCLE dispatch for unarmed crisis response. CIFD shall connect each Anchor to a FamilySource Center for community referrals. Pilot launch in three Council Districts within 120 days.

sections.title: Section 2: Anchor Network Coordination
sections.body: The Mayor's Office of Economic Development shall convene quarterly Anchor Network Convenings in each pilot Council District, bringing together designated businesses, local nonprofits, school principals, FamilySource staff, and MOCS representatives. Agendas shall be driven by participants, not pre-set by city staff. Each convening shall produce one concrete deliverable. EWDD shall maintain a public-facing dashboard tracking network activity.

sections.title: Section 3: Procurement Pathway for Anchor Businesses
sections.body: Designated Anchor businesses completing ProcureLA shall be eligible for a City Contracts Fast Track with expedited certification review and a guaranteed introductory contract opportunity. EWDD shall assign a Small Business Procurement Concierge within the nearest BusinessSource Center. A pilot micro-enterprise set-aside requiring 10 percent of eligible city contracts under $500,000 go to certified micro-enterprises shall be established in the three pilot Council Districts for 24 months.

executiveDirectives.implementation: Departments referenced in this Directive are authorized to reallocate existing resources, modify procurement timelines, and coordinate across agencies. Budget implications reported to the Mayor's Office within 30 days.
executiveDirectives.reporting: Quarterly integrated progress reports to the Mayor. First comprehensive report due 120 days after issuance.

talkingPoints._note: Three talking points per fight. Grounded in real numbers. Field rep ready.
earn: We brokered the largest school worker strike settlement in U.S. history. 30,000 LAUSD employees went from $25,000 to $33,000 in average salary. When working people earn more, families stay housed.
By 2028, every hotel worker and airport employee in Los Angeles earns at least $30 an hour. I signed that law. Tourism should not be a poverty industry in our city.

We have 14 WorkSource Centers across Los Angeles offering free career counseling, job training, and placement. If you are looking for work or looking for better work, there is a center near you, at no cost.
build: Since I took office, we have approved over 31,000 affordable units under Executive Directive 1. Los Angeles underbuilt for 50 years. We are catching up.
Over $300 million in voter-approved ULA funding is actively going toward affordable housing construction right now. Not projected. Moving.
We reformed the Adaptive Reuse process so vacant office buildings can become homes. The permitting is faster, the conversion is cheaper, and the units are coming online.
save: Because Los Angeles owns its power grid through LADWP, 4 million residents pay less for electricity than most of the rest of LA County. That is what public ownership does for working families.

If your family qualifies, you can claim up to $11,000 in EITC and CalEITC tax credits. We have free tax preparation sites across the city to help you claim every dollar you are owed. Billions go unclaimed every year. That money belongs to you.

$41.5 million goes back to low-income seniors and disabled residents every year through the Lifeline Reimbursement Program. If you or someone you know qualifies, we want to make sure you are enrolled.
care: We have 19 FamilySource Centers serving 50,000 households a year with case management, legal aid, job coaching, and benefits access. These centers are free, they are in your neighborhood, and most people who need them do not know they exist.

Homelessness in Los Angeles declined two consecutive years under my administration. That has not happened in recent city history. Inside Safe is our housing-first strategy and the numbers bear it out.

Youth Service Corps, Summer Lunch, PlayLA, SwimLA. We built and expanded programs so young Angelenos stay connected, employed, and safe when school is out. These programs are real, they are available, and we are expanding them.
protect: I signed the first overhaul of the Rent Stabilization Ordinance in nearly 40 years. Your annual rent increase is now capped at 1 to 4 percent. That protection is permanent.

Through the ULA Right to Counsel program, if you are a tenant facing eviction, you can get free legal representation in court. $57 million funds that program. You do not have to face a landlord's attorney alone.

The Tenant Anti-Harassment Ordinance protects you from intimidation and pressure tactics designed to push you out of your rent-stabilized home. It is enforceable. If your landlord is doing this to you, there are resources and we will use them.
talkingPoints.pageIntro: Concise narratives for each fight. What the Mayor says when someone asks what are you doing about affordability. These are the answers.

contact.name: Michael Green II
contact.title: Coro Fellow in Public Affairs
contact.description: Currently placed with Mayor Karen Bass's Office of Policy & Communications, working on the Affordability for All strategic communications framework and EO1 expansion.

about.projectDescription: Affordability for All is a strategic communications framework developed during a Coro Southern California Fellowship placement with Mayor Karen Bass's Office of Policy and Communications. The goal: take 209 programs across 20+ departments and build a unified narrative architecture that any Angeleno, press contact, or field representative can understand in a single conversation.
about.frameworkDescription: The Five Fights framework organizes those programs into five constituent-facing categories: Earn More, Build More, Save More, Care More, and Protect More Rights. Each fight maps to a real economic pressure Angelenos face and is backed by concrete programs, dollar figures, and measurable outcomes.
about.rubricDescription: Programs are scored on a 14-point rubric across five criteria: Bass Attribution, Scale of Impact, Communications Value, Strategic Alignment with the Affordability Agenda, and Implementation Reality. Two binary flags (Equity Depth and Durability) are noted but do not affect the score.

acknowledgments.name: Jenny Delwood
acknowledgments.role: Deputy Chief of Staff, Mayor's Office
